examples: Adenine refers to a portion of the molecule, the nitrogenous base.
This article's examples use DNA, which consists of two strands of adenine (A), cytosine (C), thymine (T), and guanine (G) nucleotides.
Glucose enters the RBC by an insulin-independent mechanism, and most is metabolized to produce ATP and reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH).
The four bases - adenine (A), guanine (G), thymine (T), and cytosine (C) - spell out the genes.
These symbols represent the four basic chemical letters, or bases, the body uses to form DNA--guanine, cytosine, adenine and thymine.
